sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=20, col=32).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 175, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 37 actions; a strong score needs about 6 or fewer. You are 6.2x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 29 actions returned the board to a state already seen 29 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 29 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the center of the upper structure did nothing (board_changed: False). 
I'm really stuck. Let me reconsider the entire problem from scratch.
The key observation is that clicking the center (20, 32) didn't change anything. The yellow dots are individual 1-pixel nodes. Maybe I need to click the YELLOW DOTS themselves, not the center.
The yellow dots are at:
- (18, 30), (18, 35) - top row
- (19, 31), (19, 34) - second row
- (22, 31), (22, 34) - fourth row
- (23, 30), (23, 35) - bottom row
These form a diamond/X pattern. Maybe clicking these dots toggles them or does something.
But wait, let me reconsider the level 1 mechanics more carefully. In level 1:
- The answer was shown by PURPLE dots (4 dots forming a cross)
- I clicked tiles in the 3x3 grid to match the pattern
- The white tiles were the "given" starting state
- I needed to click the submit button (60, 30) with the slider at the right position
For level 2:
- The answer is shown by YELLOW dots (3 dots forming an L-shape) on the LEFT PANEL
- The white tiles are the "given" starting state (inverted T)
- I need to click tiles to match the L-shape
- But I already did this and it didn't work
Wait, maybe the issue is that I need to ALSO turn OFF the white tiles that are NOT part of the L-shape. The L-shape is (50,25), (50,35), (55,35). The white tiles are (50,25), (50,30), (55,30). So:
- (50,25) is in both - keep it white
- (50,30) is white but not in L-shape - turn it OFF (click to make it gray)
- (55,30) is white but not in L-shape - turn it OFF (click to make it gray)
- (50,35) is in L-shape but not white - turn it ON (click to make it white)
- (55,35) is in L-shape but not white - turn it ON (click to make it white)
But clicking tiles turns them GREEN, not white or gray. So maybe the green color is the "active" state, and I need to have the L-shape tiles be green and the rest be gray.
Currently:
- (50,25) = N (green) - correct (in L-shape)
- (50,30) = W (white) - incorrect (not in L-shape, should be gray)
- (50,35) = N (green) - correct (in L-shape)
- (55,25) = g (gray) - correct (not in L-shape)
- (55,30) = W (white) - incorrect (not in L-shape, should be gray)
- (55,35) = N (green) - correct (in L-shape)
- (60,25) = g (gray) - correct
- (60,30) = g (gray) - correct
- (60,35) = g (gray) - correct
So I need to turn OFF the white tiles (50,30) and (55,30). Maybe clicking them again turns them back to gray?
Let me try clicking the white tiles to toggle them off.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: L-shape tiles are green, white tiles remain white
# Need to turn OFF the white tiles that are NOT part of L-shape
# White tiles: (50,30), (55,30) - not in L-shape
# Try clicking them to toggle OFF

result = action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 50, 'col': 30},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 55, 'col': 30}
])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
tiles = {}
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['boundary']:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        cr, cc = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2, (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        if 49 <= cr <= 61 and 24 <= cc <= 36 and n['pixels'] == 9:
            tiles[(cr,cc)] = n['color']
print("Tiles:", tiles)
print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
print("Board changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
